title: From Qubit to Qubit: A Graduate Course in Quantum Mechanics
abstract: This textbook is drawn from notes for a two-semester graduate course in quantum mechanics. It begins with the most constrained quantum system, and recovers the rest of the subject by relaxing those constraints one at a time. The starting point is a single qubit, the smallest nontrivial Hilbert space with the strongest possible restriction on its dynamics, made concrete by a Bloch cube whose six faces are the cardinal states of a spin-1/2 system. Tensor products admit many qubits; lattices give them a place to live; time evolution sets them in motion; the continuum limit produces wavefunctions; three-dimensional angular momentum, the hydrogen atom, and perturbation theory follow; Lorentz invariance promotes the lattice of spinors to the Dirac equation; and the renormalization group asks how theories at different scales relate. Each chapter loosens one feature of the qubit while keeping the others fixed, so that the standard apparatus of graduate quantum mechanics arrives as a sequence of controlled generalizations rather than as separate topics. Discrete-to-continuous transitions recur at four scales: in Hilbert-space dimension, in real space, in time, and in coupling. The book closes by reimposing one of the original constraints, returning to a two-level system that is now a logical qubit protected by quantum error correction, with the fault-tolerance threshold appearing as an unstable RG fixed point and supplying the reason a logical qubit, independent of its underlying hardware, can exist at all.
summary: This is a graduate-level textbook that reinterprets the standard quantum mechanics curriculum as a series of controlled generalizations starting from a single qubit. It connects fundamental quantum mechanics to advanced topics like the Dirac equation and the Renormalization Group through the lens of discrete-to-continuous transitions.
main_problem: The text aims to provide a unified pedagogical framework for graduate quantum mechanics by connecting the simplest quantum system (the qubit) to complex phenomena like the Dirac equation and the Renormalization Group.
main_result: It demonstrates that the standard apparatus of graduate quantum mechanics can be understood as a sequence of controlled generalizations, moving from discrete to continuous systems across various scales.
method: A 'looping' pedagogical approach that progressively relaxes constraints (dimensionality, space, time, and coupling) and uses the Renormalization Group to bridge microscopic and macroscopic physics.
model_or_system: The foundational model is the single spin-1/2 qubit (Bloch cube), which is expanded into multi-qubit lattices, the hydrogen atom, the Dirac equation, and the Ising model.
key_observables: Expectation values, probability (Born rule), energy eigenvalues, critical exponents, and scaling dimensions.
important_parameters: Hilbert space dimension, real space lattice spacing, time steps, coupling constants, and the epsilon parameter (4-d).
main_assumptions: Assumes a working command of undergraduate-level linear algebra, complex analysis, and multivariable calculus.
figures_summary: The Bloch cube for visualizing qubit states; the beta function for the phi^4 theory showing the Gaussian and Wilson-Fisher fixed points; and energy gaps in the transverse-field Ising model.
paper_structure: The text follows a circular logic: starting with the single qubit, moving through multi-qubit lattices and continuous wave mechanics, progressing to 3D angular momentum and the hydrogen atom, then to relativistic dynamics and the Renormalization Group, and finally returning to the protected logical qubit.
why_interesting: It provides a deep conceptual unification between quantum information (qubits/QEC) and many-body physics (RG/field theory), which is highly relevant for researchers studying the emergence of continuous physics from discrete quantum architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 10.1: Comparison of the correlation function E(θ) for the local hidden variable model and quantum mechanics. The quantum prediction −cos θ (solid blue) is stickier near perfect anti- correlation (θ = 0) and perfect correlation (θ = 180◦) than the local hidden variable model (dashed red), which departs linearly. Both agree at θ = 0◦, 90◦, and 180◦.

Figure 10.2: The four CHSH measurement directions in the xz-plane. Alice measures along two face directions of the Bloch cube (ˆa = ˆz and ˆa′ = ˆx, solid blue). Bob measures along two edge directions (ˆb at 45◦and ˆb′ at 135◦, dashed red). Adjacent directions are separated by 45◦, producing the three-close-one-far pattern: the pairs (ˆa,ˆb), (ˆa′,ˆb), and (ˆa′,ˆb′) are each 45◦apart, while (ˆa,ˆb′) is 135◦apart.

Figure 10.3: The CHSH parameter |S| as a function of the angular separation δ between adjacent measurement directions, assuming four equally spaced directions at angles 0, δ, 2δ, 3δ in the xz- plane. The quantum prediction (solid blue) exceeds the classical bound of 2 (dashed gray) for a range of angles centered on δ = 45◦, where the violation is maximal at |S| = 2 √
